subject: Mathematics
text: Matrix is the arrangements of numbers, variables, symbols, or expressions in a rectangular table that contains various numbers of rows and columns.
text_html: Matrix is the arrangements of numbers, variables, symbols, or expressions in a rectangular table that contains various numbers of rows and columns.

subject: Mathematics
text: Order of Matrix: The size of a matrix (which is known as the order of the matrix) is determined by the number of rows and columns in the matrix.
text_html: Order of Matrix: The size of a matrix (which is known as the order of the matrix) is determined by the number of rows and columns in the matrix.

subject: Mathematics
text: If a matrix has m rows and n columns, then it will have m {tex}\times{/tex} n elements. A matrix is represented by the uppercase letter.
text_html: If a matrix has m rows and n columns, then it will have m {tex}\times{/tex} n elements. A matrix is represented by the uppercase letter.

subject: Mathematics
text: Elements in the matrix are represented by the lower-case letter and two subscripts representing the position of the element in the number of row and column in the same order, in this case, 'aij', where i is the number of rows, and j is the number of columns.
text_html: Elements in the matrix are represented by the lower-case letter and two subscripts representing the position of the element in the number of row and column in the same order, in this case, 'aij', where i is the number of rows, and j is the number of columns.

subject: Mathematics
text: {tex}\begin{array}{cccccccccc}&&1&&&2&...&&n&\end{array}\\\begin{array}{c}1\\2\\3\\\vdots\\m\end{array}\begin{bmatrix}a_{11}&a_{12}&...&a_{1n}\\a_{21}&a_{22}&...&a_{2n}\\a_{31}&a_{32}&...&a_{3n}\\\vdots&\vdots&\vdots&\vdots\\a{}_{m1}&a_{m2}&...&a_{mn}\end{bmatrix}{/tex}
text_html: {tex}\begin{array}{cccccccccc}&&1&&&2&...&&n&\end{array}\\\begin{array}{c}1\\2\\3\\\vdots\\m\end{array}\begin{bmatrix}a_{11}&a_{12}&...&a_{1n}\\a_{21}&a_{22}&...&a_{2n}\\a_{31}&a_{32}&...&a_{3n}\\\vdots&\vdots&\vdots&\vdots\\a{}_{m1}&a_{m2}&...&a_{mn}\end{bmatrix}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
text: Row matrix: A matrix having one row is called row matrix. [a ij ] 1×n is a row matrix. For Example: A = [1 2 3 4] order is 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 4
text_html: Row matrix: A matrix having one row is called row matrix. [aij]1×n is a row matrix. For Example: A = [1 2 3 4] order is 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 4

subject: Mathematics
text: Column matrix: A matrix having only one column is called column matrix. [a ij ] m {tex}\times{/tex} 1 is a column matrix. For Example: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 2 \\ 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} order is 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1
text_html:
Column matrix: A matrix having only one column is called column matrix. [aij]m{tex}\times{/tex}1 is a column matrix.
For Example: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 2 \\ 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} order is 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1

subject: Mathematics
text: Square matrix: If the number of rows and the number of columns in a matrix are equal, then that matrix is called as square matrix. A m {tex}\times{/tex} n matrix is a square matrix if m = n. For Example: {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 3 & 2 \\ 3 & 3 & 4 \\ 5 & 6 & 0 \end{array}\right]_{3 \times 3}{/tex}
text_html:
Square matrix: If the number of rows and the number of columns in a matrix are equal, then that matrix is called as square matrix. A m {tex}\times{/tex} n matrix is a square matrix if m = n.
For Example: {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 3 & 2 \\ 3 & 3 & 4 \\ 5 & 6 & 0 \end{array}\right]_{3 \times 3}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
text: Diagonal matrix: If all non diagonal elements of square matrix, are zero, then it is called diagonal matrix. A = [aij] m {tex}\times{/tex} m is a diagonal matrix if a ij = 0, when i {tex}\neq{/tex} j For example: {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 5 \end{array}\right]_{3 \times 3} {/tex} {tex}B=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 9 \end{array}\right]_{2 \times 2}{/tex}
text_html:
Diagonal matrix: If all non diagonal elements of square matrix, are zero, then it is called diagonal matrix. A = [aij]m{tex}\times{/tex}m is a diagonal matrix if aij = 0, when i {tex}\neq{/tex} j
For example: {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 5 \end{array}\right]_{3 \times 3} {/tex} {tex}B=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 9 \end{array}\right]_{2 \times 2}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
text: Scalar Matrix: If all the elements in the diagonal of a diagonal matrix are equal then that matrix is called a scalar matrix. A = [a ij ] n {tex}\times{/tex} n is a scalar matrix if a ij = 0, when i {tex}\neq{/tex} j, a ij = k, (k is some constant), when i = j
text_html: Scalar Matrix: If all the elements in the diagonal of a diagonal matrix are equal then that matrix is called a scalar matrix. A = [aij]n{tex}\times{/tex}n is a scalar matrix if aij = 0, when i {tex}\neq{/tex} j, aij = k, (k is some constant), when i = j

subject: Mathematics
text: Unit matrix (Identity matrix): If all the elements of principal diagonal matrix are 1, then it is called as Unit or Identity matrix. Which is denoted by I. A = [a ij ] n {tex}\times{/tex} n is an identity matrix, if a ij = 1, when i = j, a ij = 0, when i {tex}\neq{/tex} j For example: {tex}\mathrm{I}_{3 \times 3}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]_{3 \times 3}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm I_{2 \times 2}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]_{2 \times 2}{/tex}
text_html:
Unit matrix (Identity matrix): If all the elements of principal diagonal matrix are 1, then it is called as Unit or Identity matrix. Which is denoted by I. A = [aij]n{tex}\times{/tex}n is an identity matrix, if aij = 1, when i = j, aij = 0, when i {tex}\neq{/tex} j
For example: {tex}\mathrm{I}_{3 \times 3}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]_{3 \times 3}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm I_{2 \times 2}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]_{2 \times 2}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
text: Zero or Null matrix: A matrix having all its elements zero is called zero matrix. It is denoted by O For example: {tex}O=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{array}\right]_{3 \times 3}{/tex}
text_html:
Zero or Null matrix: A matrix having all its elements zero is called zero matrix. It is denoted by O
For example: {tex}O=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{array}\right]_{3 \times 3}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
text: Upper triangular matrix: A square matrix [aij] is called an upper triangular matrix, if a ij = 0, when i > j For example: {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 5 & 7 \\ 0 & 3 & 6 \\ 0 & 0 & 5 \end{array}\right]_{3 \times 3}{/tex}
text_html:
Upper triangular matrix: A square matrix [aij] is called an upper triangular matrix, if aij = 0, when i > j
For example: {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 5 & 7 \\ 0 & 3 & 6 \\ 0 & 0 & 5 \end{array}\right]_{3 \times 3}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
text: Lower triangular matrix: A square matrix [a ij ] is called a lower triangular matrix, if a ij = 0, when i < j For example: {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 4 & 3 & 0 \\ 6 & 8 & 5 \end{array}\right]_{3 \times 3}{/tex}
text_html:
Lower triangular matrix: A square matrix [aij] is called a lower triangular matrix, if aij = 0, when i < j
For example: {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 4 & 3 & 0 \\ 6 & 8 & 5 \end{array}\right]_{3 \times 3}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
text: Two matrices A = [a ij ] and B = [b ij ] are said to be equal if they are of the same order and each element of A is equal to the corresponding element of B, that is a ij = b ij for all i and j.
text_html: Two matrices A = [aij] and B = [bij] are said to be equal if they are of the same order and each element of A is equal to the corresponding element of B, that is aij = bij for all i and j.

subject: Mathematics
text: Addition of matrices: If A = [a ij ] and B = [b ij ] are two matrices of the same order, say m {tex}\times{/tex} n. Then, the sum of the two matrices A and B is defined as a matrix C = [c ij ] m {tex}\times{/tex} n where c ij = a ij + b ij , for all possible values of i and j For example: {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 4 & 1 \\ 2 & 9 \end{array}\right]_{2 \times 2}{/tex} {tex}B=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3 & -1 \\ 0 & 4 \end{array}\right]_{2 \times 2}{/tex} {tex}A+B=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 4+3 & 1-1 \\ 2+0 & 9+4 \end{array}\right]_{2 \times 2}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 7 & 0 \\ 2 & 13 \end{array}\right]_{2 \times 2}{/tex}
text_html:
Addition of matrices: If A = [aij] and B = [bij] are two matrices of the same order, say m {tex}\times{/tex} n. Then, the sum of the two matrices A and B is defined as a matrix C = [cij]m{tex}\times{/tex}n where cij = aij + bij, for all possible values of i and j
For example: {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 4 & 1 \\ 2 & 9 \end{array}\right]_{2 \times 2}{/tex} {tex}B=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3 & -1 \\ 0 & 4 \end{array}\right]_{2 \times 2}{/tex}
{tex}A+B=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 4+3 & 1-1 \\ 2+0 & 9+4 \end{array}\right]_{2 \times 2}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 7 & 0 \\ 2 & 13 \end{array}\right]_{2 \times 2}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
text: Commutative Law: If A = [a ij ], B = [b ij ] are matrices of the same order, say m {tex}\times{/tex} n then A + B = B + A. Associative Law: For any three matrices A = [a ij ], B = [b ij ], C = [c ij ] of the same order, say m {tex}\times{/tex} n then (A + B) + C = A + (B + C). Existence of additive identity: Let A = [a ij ] be an m {tex}\times{/tex} n matrix and O be an m {tex}\times{/tex} n zero matrix, then A + O = O + A = A. The existence of additive inverse: Let A = [a ij ] m {tex}\times{/tex} n be any matrix, then we have another matrix as - A = [-a ij ] m {tex}\times{/tex} n such that A + (-A) = (-A) + A = O. So - A is the additive inverse of A or negative of A.
text_html:
Commutative Law: If A = [aij], B = [bij] are matrices of the same order, say m {tex}\times{/tex} n then A + B = B + A.
Associative Law: For any three matrices A = [aij], B = [bij], C = [cij] of the same order, say m {tex}\times{/tex} n then (A + B) + C = A + (B + C).
Existence of additive identity: Let A = [aij] be an m {tex}\times{/tex} n matrix and O be an m {tex}\times{/tex} n zero matrix, then A + O = O + A = A.
The existence of additive inverse: Let A = [aij]m{tex}\times{/tex}n be any matrix, then we have another matrix as - A = [-aij]m{tex}\times{/tex}n such that A + (-A) = (-A) + A = O. So - A is the additive inverse of A or negative of A.

subject: Mathematics
text: k(A + B) = kA + kB, where A and B are of same order, k is constant. (k + l) A = kA + lA, where k and l are constant.
text_html:
k(A + B) = kA + kB, where A and B are of same order, k is constant.
(k + l) A = kA + lA, where k and l are constant.

subject: Mathematics
text: Multiplication of a matrix by a scalar: if A = [a ij ] m {tex}\times{/tex} n is a matrix and k is a scalar, then kA is another matrix which is obtained by multiplying each element of A by the scalar k. In other words, kA = k [a ij ] m {tex}\times{/tex} n = [k (a ij )] m {tex}\times{/tex} n , that is, (i, j) th element of kA is ka ij for all possible values of i and j For example: {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 5 & 6 \\ 8 & 9 \end{array}\right]_{2 \times 2}{/tex} then {tex}4 \mathrm{~A}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 5 \times 4 & 6 \times 4 \\ 8 \times 4 & 9 \times 4 \end{array}\right]_{2 \times 2}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 20 & 24 \\ 32 & 36 \end{array}\right]_{2 \times 2}{/tex}
text_html:
Multiplication of a matrix by a scalar: if A = [aij]m{tex}\times{/tex}n is a matrix and k is a scalar, then kA is another matrix which is obtained by multiplying each element of A by the scalar k. In other words, kA = k [aij]m{tex}\times{/tex}n = [k (aij)]m{tex}\times{/tex}n, that is, (i, j)th element of kA is kaij for all possible values of i and j
For example: {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 5 & 6 \\ 8 & 9 \end{array}\right]_{2 \times 2}{/tex} then {tex}4 \mathrm{~A}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 5 \times 4 & 6 \times 4 \\ 8 \times 4 & 9 \times 4 \end{array}\right]_{2 \times 2}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 20 & 24 \\ 32 & 36 \end{array}\right]_{2 \times 2}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
text: Negative of a matrix: The negative of a matrix is denoted by -A. We define -A = (-1) A.
text_html: Negative of a matrix: The negative of a matrix is denoted by -A. We define -A = (-1) A.

subject: Mathematics
text: Difference of matrices: If A = [a ij ], B = [b ij ] are two matrices of the same order, say m {tex}\times{/tex} n, then difference A - B is defined as a matrix D = [d ij ], where d ij = a ij - b ij , for all value of i and j. In other words, D = A - B = A + (-1) B, that is sum of the matrix A and the matrix -B For example: {tex}\mathrm{A}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 1 \\ 8 & 9 \end{array}\right]_{2 \times 2} {/tex} {tex}\mathrm{~B}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 0 & -1 \\ 7 & 4 \end{array}\right]_{2 \times 2}{/tex} {tex}A-B=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1-0 & 1-1 \\ 8-7 & 9-4 \end{array}\right]_{2 \times 2}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 5 \end{array}\right]_{2 \times 2}{/tex}
text_html:
Difference of matrices: If A = [aij], B = [bij] are two matrices of the same order, say m {tex}\times{/tex} n, then difference A - B is defined as a matrix D = [dij], where dij = aij - bij, for all value of i and j. In other words, D = A - B = A + (-1) B, that is sum of the matrix A and the matrix -B
For example: {tex}\mathrm{A}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 1 \\ 8 & 9 \end{array}\right]_{2 \times 2} {/tex} {tex}\mathrm{~B}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 0 & -1 \\ 7 & 4 \end{array}\right]_{2 \times 2}{/tex}
{tex}A-B=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1-0 & 1-1 \\ 8-7 & 9-4 \end{array}\right]_{2 \times 2}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 5 \end{array}\right]_{2 \times 2}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
text: Multiplication of matrices: If A = [a ij ] m {tex}\times{/tex} n and B = [b jk ] n {tex}\times{/tex} p , then AB = C = [c ik ] m {tex}\times{/tex} p , where {tex}\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{ij}}=\sum_\limits{j=1}^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{ij}} \times \mathrm{b}_{\mathrm{ij}}{/tex} For example: {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 0 & -1 \\ 2 & 3 & 2 \end{array}\right]_{2 \times 3} {/tex} {tex}B=\left[\begin{array}{cc} -1 & 1 \\ 0 & 2 \\ 3 & -2 \end{array}\right]_{3 \times 2}{/tex} {tex}A \times B=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 \times-1+0 \times 0+-1 \times 3 & 1 \times 1+0 \times 2+-1 \times-2 \\ 2 \times-1+3 \times 0+2 \times 3 & 2 \times 1+3 \times 2+2 \times-2 \end{array}\right]_{2 \times 2}{/tex} {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} -4 & 3 \\ 4 & 4 \end{array}\right]_{2 \times 2}{/tex}
text_html:
Multiplication of matrices: If A = [aij]m{tex}\times{/tex}n and B = [bjk]n{tex}\times{/tex}p, then AB = C = [cik]m{tex}\times{/tex}p, where {tex}\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{ij}}=\sum_\limits{j=1}^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{ij}} \times \mathrm{b}_{\mathrm{ij}}{/tex}
For example: {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 0 & -1 \\ 2 & 3 & 2 \end{array}\right]_{2 \times 3} {/tex} {tex}B=\left[\begin{array}{cc} -1 & 1 \\ 0 & 2 \\ 3 & -2 \end{array}\right]_{3 \times 2}{/tex}
{tex}A \times B=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 \times-1+0 \times 0+-1 \times 3 & 1 \times 1+0 \times 2+-1 \times-2 \\ 2 \times-1+3 \times 0+2 \times 3 & 2 \times 1+3 \times 2+2 \times-2 \end{array}\right]_{2 \times 2}{/tex} {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} -4 & 3 \\ 4 & 4 \end{array}\right]_{2 \times 2}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
text: The associative law: For any three matrices A, B and C. We have (AB) C = A (BC), whenever both sides of the equality are defined The distributive law: For three matrices A, B and C. A (B + C) = AB + AC (A + B) C = AC + BC, whenever both sides of equality are defined. The existence of multiplicative identity: For every square matrix A, there exist an identity matrix of same order such that IA = AI = A
text_html:
The associative law: For any three matrices A, B and C. We have (AB) C = A (BC), whenever both sides of the equality are defined
The distributive law: For three matrices A, B and C.

  1. A (B + C) = AB + AC
  2. (A + B) C = AC + BC,

whenever both sides of equality are defined.
The existence of multiplicative identity: For every square matrix A, there exist an identity matrix of same order such that IA = AI = A

subject: Mathematics
text: If A = [a ij ] be an m {tex}\times{/tex} n matrix, then the matrix obtained by interchanging the rows and columns of A is called the transpose of A. Transpose of the matrix A is denoted by A′ or (A T ) For example: {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \\ 5 & 6 \end{array}\right]_{3 \times 2}{/tex} then {tex}\mathrm{A}^{\prime}=\mathrm{A}^{\mathrm{T}}=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 2 & 5 \\ 2 & 4 & 5 \end{array}\right]_{2 \times 3}{/tex}
text_html:
If A = [aij] be an m {tex}\times{/tex} n matrix, then the matrix obtained by interchanging the rows and columns of A is called the transpose of A. Transpose of the matrix A is denoted by A′ or (AT)
For example: {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \\ 5 & 6 \end{array}\right]_{3 \times 2}{/tex} then {tex}\mathrm{A}^{\prime}=\mathrm{A}^{\mathrm{T}}=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 2 & 5 \\ 2 & 4 & 5 \end{array}\right]_{2 \times 3}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
text: {tex}\left(\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right)^{\prime}{/tex} = A, {tex}(\mathrm{kA})^{\prime}=\mathrm{kA}^{\prime}{/tex} (where k is any constant) {tex}(A+B)^{\prime}=A^{\prime}+B^{\prime}{/tex} {tex}(A B)^{\prime}=B^{\prime} A^{\prime}{/tex}
text_html:
1. {tex}\left(\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right)^{\prime}{/tex} = A,
  2. {tex}(\mathrm{kA})^{\prime}=\mathrm{kA}^{\prime}{/tex} (where k is any constant)
  3. {tex}(A+B)^{\prime}=A^{\prime}+B^{\prime}{/tex}
  4. {tex}(A B)^{\prime}=B^{\prime} A^{\prime}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
text: Symmetric Matrix: A square matrix A = [a ij ] is said to be symmetric if A′ = A, that is, [a ij ] = [a ji ] for all possible values of i and j. For example: {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} \sqrt{3} & 2 & 3 \\ 2 & -1 & -2 \\ 3 & -2 & 5 \end{array}\right] {/tex} {tex}\quad A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} \sqrt{3} & 2 & 3 \\ 2 & -1 & -2 \\ 3 & -2 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
text_html:
Symmetric Matrix: A square matrix A = [aij] is said to be symmetric if A′ = A, that is, [aij] = [aji] for all possible values of i and j.
For example: {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} \sqrt{3} & 2 & 3 \\ 2 & -1 & -2 \\ 3 & -2 & 5 \end{array}\right] {/tex} {tex}\quad A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} \sqrt{3} & 2 & 3 \\ 2 & -1 & -2 \\ 3 & -2 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
text: Skew symmetric: A square matrix A = [a ij ] is said to be skew symmetric matrix if A′ = -A This means that all the diagonal elements of a skew symmetric matrix are zero. For example: {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & 2 & -3 \\ -2 & 0 & 1 \\ 3 & -1 & 0 \end{array}\right] {/tex} {tex}A^{\prime}=-\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & 2 & -3 \\ -2 & 0 & 1 \\ 3 & -1 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
text_html:
Skew symmetric: A square matrix A = [aij] is said to be skew symmetric matrix if A′ = -A
This means that all the diagonal elements of a skew symmetric matrix are zero.
For example: {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & 2 & -3 \\ -2 & 0 & 1 \\ 3 & -1 & 0 \end{array}\right] {/tex} {tex}A^{\prime}=-\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & 2 & -3 \\ -2 & 0 & 1 \\ 3 & -1 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
text: For any square matrix A with real number entries, A + {tex}A^{\prime}{/tex} is a symmetric matrix and A - {tex}A^{\prime}{/tex} is a skew symmetric matrix.
text_html: For any square matrix A with real number entries, A + {tex}A^{\prime}{/tex} is a symmetric matrix and A - {tex}A^{\prime}{/tex} is a skew symmetric matrix.

subject: Mathematics
text: ​​​​Any square matrix can be expressed as the sum of a symmetric and a skew symmetric matrix A = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (A + {tex}\mathrm{A}^{\prime}{/tex} ) + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (A - {tex}\mathrm{A}^{\prime}{/tex} ) where {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (A + {tex}\mathrm{A}^{\prime}{/tex} ) is symmetric and {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (A - {tex}\mathrm{A}^{\prime}{/tex} ) is skew symmetric
text_html:
​​​​Any square matrix can be expressed as the sum of a symmetric and a skew symmetric matrix
A = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (A + {tex}\mathrm{A}^{\prime}{/tex}) + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (A - {tex}\mathrm{A}^{\prime}{/tex}) where {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (A + {tex}\mathrm{A}^{\prime}{/tex}) is symmetric and {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (A - {tex}\mathrm{A}^{\prime}{/tex}) is skew symmetric

subject: Mathematics
text: If A is a square matrix of order m, and if there exists another square matrix B of the same order m, such that AB = BA = I, then B is called the inverse matrix of A and it is denoted by A -1 . In that case A is said to be invertible.
text_html: If A is a square matrix of order m, and if there exists another square matrix B of the same order m, such that AB = BA = I, then B is called the inverse matrix of A and it is denoted by A-1. In that case A is said to be invertible.

subject: Mathematics
text: A rectangular matrix does not possess inverse matrix, since for products BA and AB to be defined and to be equal, it is necessary that matrices A and B should be square matrices of the same order.
text_html: A rectangular matrix does not possess inverse matrix, since for products BA and AB to be defined and to be equal, it is necessary that matrices A and B should be square matrices of the same order.

subject: Mathematics
text: If B is the inverse of A, then A is also the inverse of B.
text_html: If B is the inverse of A, then A is also the inverse of B.

subject: Mathematics
text: Inverse of a square matrix, if it exists, is unique.
text_html: Inverse of a square matrix, if it exists, is unique.
